title: Seven Years' War
description: Practice your historical skills with questions on the Seven Year's War.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

> “This is the Country, which the French have many Years envied us, and which they have been long meditating to make themselves Masters of: They are at length come to a Resolution to attack us. . . in one of the best of those Colonies, Virginia; and in that part of it which lies on the River Ohio. . . . The French however if they find their Way to the Coast of Virginia, will easily over-run the provinces, because each Province considers itself as independence of the Rest, and the Invaders from Canada all act under one Governor; to unite 13 Provinces which fill an Extent of 1600 Miles is not easy . . . Canada must be subdued.”

>*The Maryland Gazette*, 1755

**The excerpt is describing which of the following conflicts in the 1700s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Seven Years’ War (the French and Indian War)
The American Revolution
The French Revolution
Metacom’s War

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

> “This is the Country, which the French have many Years envied us, and which they have been long meditating to make themselves Masters of: They are at length come to a Resolution to attack us. . . in one of the best of those Colonies, Virginia; and in that part of it which lies on the River Ohio. . . . The French however if they find their Way to the Coast of Virginia, will easily over-run the provinces, because each Province considers itself as independence of the Rest, and the Invaders from Canada all act under one Governor; to unite 13 Provinces which fill an Extent of 1600 Miles is not easy. . . . Canada must be subdued.”

>*The Maryland Gazette*, 1755

**According to the excerpt, what caused the conflict between the French and the British?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: competing claims to colonial land
invasion of French troops into Britain
British support of French revolutionaries
British encroachment into colonial Florida

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

> “This is the Country, which the French have many Years envied us, and which they have been long meditating to make themselves Masters of: They are at length come to a Resolution to attack us. . . in one of the best of those Colonies, Virginia; and in that part of it which lies on the River Ohio. . . . The French however if they find their Way to the Coast of Virginia, will easily over-run the provinces, because each Province considers itself as independence of the Rest, and the Invaders from Canada all act under one Governor; to unite 13 Provinces which fill an Extent of 1600 Miles is not easy. . . . Canada must be subdued.”

>*The Maryland Gazette*, 1755

**Which of the following was an immediate result of the conflict described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: France lost major territorial claims in colonial North America
British colonists could expand their colonies to present-day Missouri
Spanish territory in North America was reduced to that of present-day Mexico
Britain began practicing salutary neglect as a reward for the colonies’ victory in the war

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the table below to answer the question. 

|| **BRITISH NATIONAL DEBT, 1739-1775 (£ Sterling)** ||
Year | National Debt 
- | - 
1739  |  46,954,623
1748  |  78,293,313
1755  |  74,571,849
1762  |  146,682,844
1775  |  135,943,051

> -Source: Sir John Sinclair, historian, *The History of the Public Revenue of the British Empire*, 1966

**Which of the following was a significant cause of the trend from 1755-1762 shown in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: costs of involvement in the Seven Years’ War with France
costs of increased colonization of the West Indies
costs of involvement in colonial conflicts with indigenous nations
costs of increased demand for enslaved people in the British sugar islands

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the table below to answer the question. 

|| **BRITISH NATIONAL DEBT, 1739-1775 (£ Sterling)** ||

1739  |  46,954,623

1755  |  74,571,849
1762  |  146,682,844
1775  |  135,943,051

> -Source: Sir John Sinclair, historian, *The History of the Public Revenue of the British Empire*, 1966

**Which of the following was a direct effect of the large national debt shown in 1762?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the creation of new taxes on the American colonies
the elimination of taxes for all British citizens
the end of the Navigation Acts
the sale of the Northwest Territory

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the table below to answer the question. 

|| **BRITISH NATIONAL DEBT, 1739-1775 (£ Sterling)** ||
Year | National Debt 
- | - 
1739  |  46,954,623
1748  |  78,293,313
1755  |  74,571,849
1762  |  146,682,844
1775  |  135,943,051

>-Source: Sir John Sinclair, historian, *The History of the Public Revenue of the British Empire*, 1966

**Which of the following pieces of legislation passed after 1762 as a reaction to the changes illustrated in the chart?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Townshend Acts
The Navigation Acts
The Albany Plan of Union
The Intolerable Acts

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Niagara at most should be my furthest Post in that quarter . . . By this Means we may keep up a Trade with the most distant Nations, retain their good Opinion, and totally prevent any Jealousy of our intending them any ill. . . . As we increase in Numbers on this Continent, it’s easy and Safe to advance our settlements in Townships, though this I would do only by Cession or by Purchase of the Lands to prevent the Shadow of an Excuse for the Indians to quarrel with us.”

>-Source: Colonel William Eyre, as printed in *The First Global War: Britain, France, and the Fate of North America, 1756-1775,* 1764

**The excerpt is best understood as a response to which of the following historical developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the acquisition of new territory from France following the Seven Years’ War
the institution of new taxes on the colonies to pay for global conflicts
the decision to enforce the Navigation Acts in the colonies
the creation of alliances with indigenous nations to fight the French and Spanish

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below.

![The map shows the settled area in 1763. There is a harsh line separating indigenous territory and the American colonies, running parallel along the Appalachian Mountains.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fd5df5ca64ea9d963e7b440ed2fd26df.svg)

**The map most directly depicts the ways in which the British government attempted to do which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: limit the westward expansion of British colonial settlement
improve trade relationships with the colonists
remove indigenous nations from North America
restrict the eastward expansion of indigenous people

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below.

![The map shows the settled area in 1763. There is a harsh line separating indigenous territory and the American colonies, running parallel along the Appalachian Mountains.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fd5df5ca64ea9d963e7b440ed2fd26df.svg)

**Which of the following statements best explains the reason that the British government passed the Proclamation of 1763?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: to limit costly conflicts over access to land between American colonists and indigenous communities
to reduce conflicts between the American colonists and indigenous people after Bacon’s Rebellion
to punish the American colonists for their lack of involvement in the Seven Years’ War
to discourage interracial relationships between indigenous people and British colonists

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below.

![The map shows the settled area in 1763. There is a harsh line separating indigenous territory and the American colonies, running parallel along the Appalachian Mountains.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fd5df5ca64ea9d963e7b440ed2fd26df.svg)
**Which of the following best describes the effect of the Proclamation of 1763 on the relationship between Britain and the American colonies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Tensions increased between Britain and the American colonies over territorial expansion.
Tensions decreased between Britain and the American colonies over colonial conflicts with indigenous communities.
Tensions decreased between Britain and the American colonies over the role of colonial governments.
Tensions increased between Britain and the American colonies over taxation without representation.

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below.

![Proclamation of 1763](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0bdcf7c1c66e99c61e10ad3d41962d24.png)
A map showing the colonies in 1775, with a demarcation line from the Proclamation of 1763. Image credit: [Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Map_of_territorial_growth_1775.svg)

**Which of the following best describes the most immediate impact of the Proclamation of 1763 on the British North American colonies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Colonists disobeyed the Proclamation of 1763, moving westward past the Appalachian Mountains.
Colonists refused to pay British taxes until Parliament repealed the Proclamation of 1763.
Colonists abided by the Proclamation of 1763, not moving into newly-acquired territory.
Colonists began capturing and enslaving indigenous people in retaliation against the Proclamation.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Niagara at most should be my furthest Post in that quarter . . . By this Means we may keep up a Trade with the most distant Nations, retain their good Opinion, and totally prevent any Jealousy of our intending them any ill. . . . As we increase in Numbers on this Continent, it’s easy and Safe to advance our settlements in Townships, though this I would do only by Cession or by Purchase of the Lands to prevent the Shadow of an Excuse for the Indians to quarrel with us.”

>-Source: Colonel William Eyre, as printed in *The First Global War: Britain, France, and the Fate of North America, 1756-1775,* 1764

**The ideas expressed in the excerpt emerged most directly from a larger debate over British Parliament’s decision to:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: limit westward expansion beyond the Appalachian Mountains.
tax British-made goods, making them more expensive for the colonists.
declare war on the French over territorial boundaries.
force neighboring indigenous nations to convert to Christianity.
